food_subgroup: Teochew clear rock-sugar sweet soup with longan, barley, lotus seeds, ginkgo nuts, white fungus, sago, and dried persimmon

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for Cheng Tng. Closest parent would be a generic sweet soup or dessert soup concept. No code assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Teochew clear sweet soup with mixed dried fruits — closest parent match in FoodEx2; no exact code exists for Cheng Tng
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0GBQ is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor for a mixed-ingredient Asian sweet soup. Exact code for Cheng Tng does not exist in FoodEx2 as of 2026.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for Cheng Tng or Teochew sweet soup as a food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker-centre bowl of Cheng Tng is approximately 350g, providing around 160 kcal, 28g sugar, 0.5g fat, and 20mg sodium.

nutrient_source.title: Cheng Tng — HPB Singapore (Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Cheng Tng. Values represent the composite dish as served at Singapore hawker centres. Copper, manganese, and vitamin K values supplemented from enriched_json cross-reference with USDA FoodData Central component data for individual ingredients (dried longan, barley, white fungus) where HPB did not report these micronutrients.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Composite Asian Sweet Soups — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for pearl barley (GI 28), sucrose/rock sugar (GI 65), and dried fruit components
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Cheng Tng in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI ~55 is a composite estimate: pearl barley (GI 28, University of Sydney GI Database) lowers the composite; rock sugar (sucrose equivalent, GI ~65) and dried longan/red dates (high natural sugar, GI ~60–65) raise it. Weighted composite across ingredient proportions yields GI ~55. GL per serving = (55 × 28g carbohydrate per 350g serving) / 100 = 15.4. This is consistent with the input record's GL of 13.75 (minor rounding difference due to serving size used). Confidence: 0.9.

hypertension.guidance: Cheng Tng is one of the lowest-sodium Singapore hawker desserts at approximately 20mg sodium per bowl, making it an excellent choice for individuals managing hypertension; the only cardiovascular consideration is the 28g sugar per serving, which should be factored into overall daily intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: GI ~55 (low), GL ~15.4 per bowl; 28g sugar per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Cheng Tng has a low GI (~55) and a glycemic load of approximately 15 per bowl, making it one of the more glycaemically favourable Singapore hawker desserts; however, the 28g sugar per serving still represents a meaningful postprandial glucose load and individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume it in moderation, preferably as an occasional treat rather than a daily dessert.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.18g per bowl), zero trans fat, extremely low sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Cheng Tng is very low in saturated fat (0.18g per bowl), contains no trans fat, and is extremely low in sodium (~20mg per bowl), presenting minimal cardiovascular risk from fat or salt; barley beta-glucans may provide a modest LDL-lowering benefit (Grade B evidence), though the 28g sugar per bowl warrants consideration in the context of long-term cardiometabolic health.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Low fat, potential anti-inflammatory components (barley beta-glucans, white fungus polysaccharides)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Cheng Tng is very low in fat and contains barley beta-glucans (Grade B anti-inflammatory evidence for LDL reduction) and white fungus (Tremella fuciformis) polysaccharides with studied immunomodulatory properties (Grade C, limited clinical evidence); the 28g sugar per bowl is mildly pro-inflammatory and should be considered in the context of overall dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cheng Tng (清湯, literally 'clear soup') is a classic Teochew dessert soup originating from the Teochew (Chaozhou) community and widely popular in Singapore and Malaysia. It is a light, refreshing, multi-textured sweet soup prized in Chinese traditional medicine for its 'cooling' (qing re) properties. The clear broth is prepared by slow-simmering dried longans, red dates, and rock sugar, often with pandan leaf for fragrance. The soup is loaded with a variety of ingredients: pearl barley, white fungus (Tremella fuciformis), ginkgo nuts, lotus seeds, sago pearls, sweet potato cubes, and dried persimmon, creating a harmonious blend of chewy, soft, and slightly crunchy textures. It is served either piping hot or chilled over ice, making it suitable for any weather. Per standard hawker-centre bowl (~350g): approximately 160 kcal, 0.57g protein per 100g, 0.14g fat per 100g, 8g carbohydrate per 100g (all sugar), 1g dietary fibre per 100g, and only 5.7mg sodium per 100g (~20mg per bowl). GI ~55 (low), GL ~15 per bowl. CLINICAL NOTE: Ginkgo nuts (3–5 per standard serving) are within safe limits for adults; limit to fewer than 10 per serving for adults and fewer than 5 for children and pregnant women, as ginkgotoxin (4-O-methylpyridoxine) is a heat-stable vitamin B6 antagonist. Cheng Tng is one of the lowest-sodium and most glycaemically favourable Singapore hawker desserts. Source: HPB Singapore (Foodo). Confidence: high.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (approved primary source for Singapore dishes). GI estimated as analogous composite from University of Sydney GI Database component values for pearl barley and sucrose, with documented estimation basis. Micronutrients copper, manganese, and vitamin K supplemented from USDA FoodData Central component cross-reference where HPB did not report these values.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Cheng Tng is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central for this composite dish as of 2026-04-14. 2. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the composite dish as served is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database as of 2026-04-14. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo). 4. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — this is a plant-based vegan dessert soup with no animal-derived ingredients). 5. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — entirely plant-based dish with no animal products). 6. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — no vitamin D-containing ingredients). 7. GI ~55 is a composite analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Cheng Tng exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026-04-14. 8. Copper, manganese, and vitamin K values (confidence 0.9) were supplemented from enriched_json cross-reference with USDA FoodData Central component data for individual ingredients. 9. Portions: the canonical serving size is 1 bowl = 350g per HPB Singapore. A secondary 300g serving size appears in the input but is not used as the default. 10. Ginkgo nut safety: ginkgotoxin (4-O-methylpyridoxine) is heat-stable; safe limit is fewer than 10 nuts per serving for adults, fewer than 5 for children and pregnant women. 11. GL per serving recalculated as (55 × 28g carbohydrate per 350g serving) / 100 = 15.4; input record stated 13.75 using a slightly different carbohydrate figure — 15.4 is used here based on 8g carbohydrate per 100g × 350g = 28g total carbohydrate. 12. Aliases consistency check (q8): all strings in aliases_by_language verified to appear verbatim in flat aliases array. 'Qīng Tāng' added to flat aliases to match romanized entry in aliases_by_language.
